title: Carved cherry tea spoon
subtitle: Small bowl spoon in green cherry for tea and condiments
excerpt: A short tea spoon carved in green cherry. Shallow oval bowl, straight handle, raw-linseed finish. An entry-level spoon-carving project that introduces the hook knife without the depth demands of a larger eating spoon.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III on treen forms. Beeton's Book of Household Management on household spoon use.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven timber, still high in moisture. Carves more easily than seasoned wood under hand tools.
A short length of round or split timber, ready to be worked into a blank for carving.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.
The safe default cut: blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, off the end of the workpiece into open air.
A short controlled cut pivoted by the off-hand thumb on the back of the blade. Used for fine shaping where blade-travel is small.
A short straight-bladed knife with a stout handle; the Swedish school-craft standard for whittling and spoon-carving.
A curved-bladed knife for hollowing the bowl of a spoon. Available in small, medium, and large radius variants.

content.text: A tea spoon is the smallest hollow-bowl spoon worth making: a short shallow oval bowl on a straight handle, 15 cm in all. Cherry is an excellent first spoon wood; it carves cleanly
green
, the grain is fine, and the finished surface takes a linseed finish with a warm glow. The hook knife work here is shallow, which makes this a better entry point than a deep eating-spoon bowl.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood
Billet

: 16 cm long, 5 cm wide, 3 cm deep. Cherry from community-orchard prunings or a tree-surgeon offcut. Sycamore or birch are close substitutes. Cherry has a slight tendency to check at the end-grain: seal the end-grain with PVA glue before setting the billet aside if you are not carving the same day.
Tools
A
sloyd knife
for the outside shaping; a small
hook knife
for hollowing the bowl; a carving axe for the rough blank (optional); a leather strop; raw linseed oil.
Method

Draw the spoon outline on the flat face of the billet: 10 cm handle, 6 cm oval bowl. Mark the bowl on the top face, then mark the side profile showing the drop from the handle level down to the bowl floor (about 8 mm below the handle line). The blank's shape, with waste removed, is a rough spoon in two dimensions.

Axe or knife the side profile to shape. Work from the bowl shoulder toward the bowl end, then from the bowl shoulder toward the handle end. Leave the handle section square at first; refine it after the bowl is roughed out.
Rough out the outside of the bowl with
push cuts
, cutting from the rim of the bowl down toward the bottom of the blank. Establish the bowl perimeter first, then remove the waste from the center.
Shape the handle to its final oval cross-section, using
thumb-pivot cuts
at the shoulder transition. Aim for 10 mm wide and 7 mm thick at the shoulder, narrowing to 8 mm wide and 5 mm thick at the end.
Hollow the bowl with the small hook knife. The hook knife cuts on a pull; brace the spoon
blank
firmly in the four-finger grip and draw the knife toward you in short arcs. Remove material in thin layers. The finished bowl should be 4 mm deep at the center and 4 mm wall-thickness all round.
Smooth the bowl interior with the hook knife tip, working around the perimeter. The goal is an even concave curve with no flat spots. A flat spot catches the tongue when the spoon is in use.
Leave to dry for 2 weeks on a shelf before finishing.
Finishing

Sand the handle and outside of the bowl through 120 and 180 grit. Leave the inside of the bowl as cut if the surface is clean; a hook-knife-polished bowl reads better than a sanded one. Apply raw linseed oil, cure 2 weeks before food contact.
Variations
Longer handle for a honey spoon: extend the handle to 14 cm and taper it more toward the end.
Left-handed bowl: most tea spoon bowls are symmetrical, but if the bowl curves toward one side, mirror the curve for a left-handed version.
Care
Wash under warm running water with a small amount of washing-up liquid. Towel-dry. Never soak and never dishwasher. Re-oil every ten to fifteen washes.

items.symptom: Bowl interior has ridges from the hook knife
items.cause: Cuts taken at different depths in adjacent passes
items.fix: Take overlapping passes in one direction, then cross-cut at 90 degrees. Finish with the hook knife tip working in a tight spiral from the center outward.

items.symptom: Bowl wall splits as it dries
items.cause: Uneven wall thickness; one section thinner than the others
items.fix: Check wall thickness by pressing lightly on the bowl edge before drying. Thin spots flex noticeably. Correct with the hook knife before setting aside.

items.symptom: Grain tears on the outside of the bowl
items.cause: Cutting across the grain on the convex surface
items.fix: On a spoon bowl, the grain always runs from the handle toward the bowl tip. Cut from the shoulder of the bowl toward the bowl tip, not from tip toward shoulder.
